text:
None may teach it aught,
'T is the seal, despair, --
An imperial affliction
Sent to us from the air.
source_text:
None may teach it anything,
'T is the seal, despair, --
An imperial affliction
Sent us of the air.

text:
The bat is dusked with wrinkled wings,
Like some fallow relic,
And not a note suffuses his lips,
Or none that may be heard.
source_text:
The bat is dun with wrinkled wings
Like fallow article,
And not a song pervades his lips,
Or none perceptible.

text:
And who art thou, melancholy shade?  
Gigantic, visionary, thyself a vision,  
With sovereign limbs and pious, shining eyes,  
Around thee, with each glance, a golden world thou spreadst,  
Investing it with glorious hues.
source_text:
And who art thou sad shade?
Gigantic, visionary, thyself a visionary,
With majestic limbs and pious beaming eyes,
Spreading around with every look of thine a golden world,
Enhuing it with gorgeous hues.

text:
MAMMON:
This
For Queen Jona would suffice, and less;
But ’tis the swinish multitude I dread,
And in that dread I have—
source_text:
MAMMON:
This
For Queen Jona would suffice, and less;
But ’tis the Swinish multitude I fear,
And in that fear I have—

text:
It’s o’er yon heathy mountain,  
An’ doon yon scraggy glen,  
We daurna gae a-milking,  
For Charlie and his men,  
An’ Charlie, &c.
source_text:
It’s up yon heathery mountain,
An’ down yon scroggie glen,
We daur na gang a milking,
For Charlie and his men,
An’ Charlie, &c.

text:
It knows no future save itself,  
Within its boundless realms abide  
Its past, made clear, to apprehend  
Fresh epochs yet of pain.
source_text:
It has no future but itself,
Its infinite realms contain
Its past, enlightened to perceive
New periods of pain.

text:
When America fulfills what was promised,
When through these States there stride a hundred millions of superb persons,
When the remainder parts aside for superb persons and yields to them,
When breeds of the most perfect mothers betoken America,
Then to me and mine our rightful fruition.
source_text:
When America does what was promis’d,
When through these States walk a hundred millions of superb persons,
When the rest part away for superb persons and contribute to them,
When breeds of the most perfect mothers denote America,
Then to me and mine our due fruition.

text:
May’st thou long, sweet crimson gem,  
Richly grace thy native stem;  
Till some eve, serene and sober,  
With dews descending, breath of odor,  
While all around the woodland rings,  
And every bird thy requiem sings;  
Thou, amid the dirgelike sound,  
Scatter’st thy dying honors round,  
And yield to parent Earth once more  
The loveliest form she ever bore.
source_text:
May’st thou long, sweet crimson gem,
Richly deck thy native stem;
Till some ev’ning, sober, calm,
Dropping dews, and breathing balm,
While all around the woodland rings,
And ev’ry bird thy requiem sings;
Thou, amid the dirgeful sound,
Shed thy dying honours round,
And resign to parent Earth
The loveliest form she e’er gave birth.

text:
Wearied and woe-forlorn,  
Raspy with lament outworn,  
Arm in arm, for seven days  
They trod the desert ways.
source_text:
Tired and woe-begone,
Hoarse with making moan,
Arm in arm, seven days
They traced the desert ways.

text:
Another—‘Let him shear his head!
Where’s Dr. Willis?—Is he joking?
What means the rogue, and what could he hope,
No longer aping Pope,
In that barbarian Shakespeare poking?’
source_text:
Another—‘Let him shave his head!
Where’s Dr. Willis?—Or is he joking?
What does the rascal mean or hope,
No longer imitating Pope,
In that barbarian Shakespeare poking?’

text:
Oh what may vex thee, Knight at arms  
So gaunt, and so forlorn?  
The Squirrel's storehouse overflows  
And the harvest's borne.
source_text:
Oh what can ail thee Knight at arms
So haggard, and so woe begone?
The Squirrel's granary is full
And the harvest's done.

text:
Now shall I do naught but hearken,  
To gather all I hear into this song, to let sounds lend their part thereto.
source_text:
Now I will do nothing but listen,
To accrue what I hear into this song, to let sounds contribute toward it.

text:
I woke and chid my faithful fingers, --
The jewel had flown;
And now an amethystine remnant
Is all I own.
source_text:
I woke and chid my honest fingers, --
The gem was gone;
And now an amethyst remembrance
Is all I own.

text:
That heart, where various follies met,
Yet firmly to Honour still held true:
To prove Clarinda’s dearest friend,
Was all a lover well might do.
source_text:
That heart, where motley follies blend,
Was sternly still to Honour true:
To prove Clarinda’s fondest friend,
Was what a lover sure might do.

text:
“An’ warn him—what I winna name—
To bide content wi’ yowes at hame;
An’ no to rin an’ wear his cloots,
Like ither menseless, graceless brutes.
source_text:
“An’ warn him—what I winna name—
To stay content wi’ yowes at hame;
An’ no to rin an’ wear his cloots,
Like ither menseless, graceless brutes.

text:
Of sights of Nature, meads and mountains,
Of heavens so fair after the tempest, and by night the moon so
otherworldly bright,
Beaming softly, beaming down, where we dig the trenches and
pile the heaps,
I dream, I dream, I dream.
source_text:
Of scenes of Nature, fields and mountains,
Of skies so beauteous after a storm, and at night the moon so
unearthly bright,
Shining sweetly, shining down, where we dig the trenches and
gather the heaps,
I dream, I dream, I dream.

text:
INDIAN:
I lay my hand on thy pale temples!
I breathe my soul upon thee!
And if my prayers might profit,
All my joy should be
Dead, and I would live to weep,
So thou mightst win one hour of peaceful sleep.
source_text:
INDIAN:
I touch thy temples pale!
I breathe my soul on thee!
And could my prayers avail,
All my joy should be
Dead, and I would live to weep,
So thou mightst win one hour of quiet sleep.

text:
Auto-da-fé and judgment  
Are naught to the bee;  
His sundering from his rose  
To him is misery.
source_text:
Auto-da-fe and judgment
Are nothing to the bee;
His separation from his rose
To him seems misery.

text:
The forms arise!
The form measured, sawed, hewn, joined, stain’d,
The coffin-form for the dead to rest within his shroud,
The form set forth in posts, in the bedstead posts, in the posts of
the bride’s bed,
The form of the little trough, the form of the rockers beneath,
the form of the babe’s cradle,
The form of the floor-planks, the floor-planks for dancers’ feet,
The form of the planks of the household home, the home of the kindly
parents and children,
The form of the roof of the home of the glad young man and
woman, the roof above the well-wedded young man and woman,
The roof above the supper joyously cook’d by the chaste wife, and joyously
eaten by the chaste husband, content after his day’s work.
source_text:
The shapes arise!
The shape measur’d, saw’d, jack’d, join’d, stain’d,
The coffin-shape for the dead to lie within in his shroud,
The shape got out in posts, in the bedstead posts, in the posts of
the bride’s bed,
The shape of the little trough, the shape of the rockers beneath,
the shape of the babe’s cradle,
The shape of the floor-planks, the floor-planks for dancers’ feet,
The shape of the planks of the family home, the home of the friendly
parents and children,
The shape of the roof of the home of the happy young man and
woman, the roof over the well-married young man and woman,
The roof over the supper joyously cook’d by the chaste wife, and joyously
eaten by the chaste husband, content after his day’s work.

text:
Oh, cease! must hate and death return?
Cease! must men slay and die?
Cease! drain not to the very dregs the urn
Of bitter prophecy.
The world is weary of the past,
Oh, might it sink to rest at last!
source_text:
Oh, cease! must hate and death return?
Cease! must men kill and die?
Cease! drain not to its dregs the urn
Of bitter prophecy.
The world is weary of the past,
Oh, might it die or rest at last!

text:
I behold the Brazilian vaquero,  
I behold the Bolivian climbing Mount Sorata,  
I behold the Wacho crossing the plains, I behold the peerless rider of  
steeds, his lariat looped upon his arm,  
I behold, over the pampas, the chase of wild cattle for their hides.
source_text:
I see the Brazilian vaquero,
I see the Bolivian ascending mount Sorata,
I see the Wacho crossing the plains, I see the incomparable rider of
horses with his lasso on his arm,
I see over the pampas the pursuit of wild cattle for their hides.

text:
I never spake with God,  
Nor walked the halls of Heaven;  
Yet sure am I of that dear place  
As though the chart were given.
source_text:
I never spoke with God,
Nor visited in heaven;
Yet certain am I of the spot
As if the chart were given.

text:
Till we came unto the steadfast town,  
No man, it seemed, he knew;  
And, bowing with a mighty mien  
Toward me, the sea withdrew.
source_text:
Until we met the solid town,
No man he seemed to know;
And bowing with a mighty look
At me, the sea withdrew.

text:
Gay, gay little sparrow!
Beneath the leaves so green
A glad-eyed blossom
Sees thee, swift as arrow,
Seek thy narrow cradle,
Close unto my bosom.
source_text:
Merry, merry sparrow!
Under leaves so green
A happy blossom
Sees you, swift as arrow,
Seek your cradle narrow,
Near my bosom.

text:
Then, turning from them, in reverence,
“Their bed-time now,” she said;
“The bumble-bees shall wake them
When April woods burn red.”
source_text:
Then, turning from them, reverent,
"Their bed-time 't is," she said;
"The bumble-bees will wake them
When April woods are red."

text:
Their schemes and cunning hae unstrung me,
They’ve cheated me, and a’ that;
But stand ye back, and here comes—“The Sex!”
I’m still for them for a’ that.
source_text:
Their tricks an’ craft hae put me daft,
They’ve taen me in, an’ a’ that;
But clear your decks, and here’s—“The Sex!”
I like the jads for a’ that.

text:
MALPIGLIO:
Lady Leonora must never know
That I have penned a sonnet to her praise,
Wherein I ... Venus and Adonis.
You must not take my gold and not serve me.
source_text:
MALPIGLIO:
The Lady Leonora cannot know
That I have written a sonnet to her fame,
In which I ... Venus and Adonis.
You should not take my gold and serve me not.

text:
The businessman, the great accumulator,
After patient years of weighing returns, making ready for his going,
Bequeaths houses and lands to his children, leaves stocks, goods,
money for a school or a hospital,
Leaves sums to certain companions to purchase keepsakes, souvenirs of gems
and gold.
source_text:
The business man the acquirer vast,
After assiduous years surveying results, preparing for departure,
Devises houses and lands to his children, bequeaths stocks, goods,
funds for a school or hospital,
Leaves money to certain companions to buy tokens, souvenirs of gems
and gold.

text:
And she beheld princes lying low beneath the sheen  
Of sun-bright jewels; and about each temple court  
In dormitories set, in rank on rank, she seen  
The priests asleep—all fashioned in one sort—  
For all had been taught to be so. And in the green  
Of peasants’ huts, and in the harbor’s port,  
She saw the sailors rocked upon the waves,  
And the dead hushed softly in their dreamless graves.
source_text:
And she saw princes couched under the glow
Of sunlike gems; and round each temple-court
In dormitories ranged, row after row,
She saw the priests asleep—all of one sort—
For all were educated to be so.—
The peasants in their huts, and in the port
The sailors she saw cradled on the waves,
And the dead lulled within their dreamless graves.

text:
There is no pause, and there can never be a pause,
If I, you, and the worlds, and all that lies beneath or above their skins,
were, in this instant, brought back to a pale drifting state, it would
not matter in the long run,
We would most certainly rise again where we now are,
And surely move still farther, and then farther and farther.
source_text:
There is no stoppage and never can be stoppage,
If I, you, and the worlds, and all beneath or upon their surfaces,
were this moment reduced back to a pallid float, it would
not avail the long run,
We should surely bring up again where we now stand,
And surely go as much farther, and then farther and farther.

text:
But a sickness soon sank into
The very life and soul of Peter—
He moved about—he slept—he wore the hue
Of health upon his cheeks—and few
Could dig more stoutly—none a heartier eater.
source_text:
But a disease soon struck into
The very life and soul of Peter—
He walked about—slept—had the hue
Of health upon his cheeks—and few
Dug better—none a heartier eater.

text:
So still, oh, how still it was!  
That no one there could know  
But that the little figure  
Rocked gentler, to and fro?
source_text:
So quiet, oh, how quiet!
That nobody might know
But that the little figure
Rocked softer, to and fro?

text:
A train rolled past the burial gate,
A bird sprang up and sang,
And twittered, shivered, and shook his throat
Till all the churchyard rang;
source_text:
A train went through a burial gate,
A bird broke forth and sang,
And trilled, and quivered, and shook his throat
Till all the churchyard rang;

text:
As though it lingered ever;
And yet its entire course
Is briefer than a serpent’s pause,
And swifter than a tear.
source_text:
As if it tarried always;
And yet its whole career
Is shorter than a snake's delay,
And fleeter than a tare.

text:
The council of the Gods is met,  
Each in his place and rank is set;  
There is hush within the spaces—  
Lo! Satan, Christ, and Mahomet  
Spring from their places!
source_text:
The senate of the Gods is met,
Each in his rank and station set;
There is silence in the spaces—
Lo! Satan, Christ, and Mahomet
Start from their places!

text:
How dismal it is to be someone!
How exposed, like a frog
To call your name all the long day
To a reverent bog!
source_text:
How dreary to be somebody!
How public, like a frog
To tell your name the livelong day
To an admiring bog!

text:
PAGE 21. l. 320. Adonian feast. Adonis, that lovely youth beloved by Venus,  
was struck down by a wild boar in the hunt;  
then Venus bore him onward to Elysium,  
where he rests, his head pillowed on flowers.  
Cf. Endymion, ii. 387.
source_text:
PAGE 21. l. 320. Adonian feast. Adonis was a beautiful youth beloved
of Venus. He was killed by a wild boar when hunting, and Venus then had
him borne to Elysium, where he sleeps pillowed on flowers. Cf.
Endymion, ii. 387.

text:
Morn passed away, then noon, then evening, and at last night fell,  
And we prolonged our tranquil talk beneath the sphere  
Of the still moon—when all at once was mingled  
With our repose a dim and nameless fear;  
And from the cave behind I seemed to hear  
Voices ascending!—broken, imperfect sounds,  
And smothered shrieks,—and now, more near, more near,  
A tumult and a trampling of crowded feet  
That beat through the secret hollows of the earth beneath.
source_text:
Morn fled, noon came, evening, then night descended,
And we prolonged calm talk beneath the sphere
Of the calm moon—when suddenly was blended
With our repose a nameless sense of fear;
And from the cave behind I seemed to hear
Sounds gathering upwards!—accents incomplete,
And stifled shrieks,—and now, more near and near,
A tumult and a rush of thronging feet
The cavern’s secret depths beneath the earth did beat.

text:
Dear friend, whoever you are, receive this kiss,  
I give it to you especially; do not forget me,  
I feel like one who has finished the day’s labor, and would rest awhile,  
I receive again my many translations, from my avataras  
rising upward, while others surely wait for me,  
An unknown realm, more real than I had dreamed, more immediate, casts  
its waking rays about me. Farewell!  
Remember what I have said; I may yet come back,  
I love you, I depart from materials,  
I am as one disembodied, triumphant, dead.
source_text:
Dear friend whoever you are take this kiss,
I give it especially to you, do not forget me,
I feel like one who has done work for the day to retire awhile,
I receive now again of my many translations, from my avataras
ascending, while others doubtless await me,
An unknown sphere more real than I dream’d, more direct, darts
awakening rays about me, So long!
Remember my words, I may again return,
I love you, I depart from materials,
I am as one disembodied, triumphant, dead.

text:
The Black man grips the reins of his four horses, the block swings
beneath on its looped chain,
The Black man driving the long dray from the stone-yard, firm and
upright he stands balanced on one leg on the string-piece,
His blue shirt shows his broad neck and chest and falls loose over
his hip-band,
His look is tranquil and masterful, he brushes the droop of his hat
back from his brow,
The sun strikes his wiry hair and mustache, strikes the dark sheen of
his polished, flawless limbs.
source_text:
The negro holds firmly the reins of his four horses, the block swags
underneath on its tied-over chain,
The negro that drives the long dray of the stone-yard, steady and
tall he stands pois’d on one leg on the string-piece,
His blue shirt exposes his ample neck and breast and loosens over
his hip-band,
His glance is calm and commanding, he tosses the slouch of his hat
away from his forehead,
The sun falls on his crispy hair and mustache, falls on the black of
his polish’d and perfect limbs.

text:
I got your letter, and the birds’;
The maples never knew
That you were coming, — I declare,
How bright their faces grew!
But, March, forgive me —
And all those hills
You left for me to hue;
There was no purple fit enough,
You carried it all with you.
source_text:
I got your letter, and the birds';
The maples never knew
That you were coming, -- I declare,
How red their faces grew!
But, March, forgive me --
And all those hills
You left for me to hue;
There was no purple suitable,
You took it all with you.

text:
Are you the one who would take a place to teach, or to call yourself a poet here in  
these States?  
The place is venerable, the conditions hard and unyielding.
source_text:
Are you he who would assume a place to teach or be a poet here in
the States?
The place is august, the terms obdurate.

text:
And how to each Immortal, every one,  
A share was given of all that is and lives;  
But most Mnemosyne did Maia’s son  
Endue with light through all his mighty hymns;—  
And, as each God was born or newly known,  
He sang their birth and being in due sorts and rises,  
And so in ordered measure did he move  
Apollo unto love too deep for speech.
source_text:
And how to the Immortals every one
A portion was assigned of all that is;
But chief Mnemosyne did Maia’s son
Clothe in the light of his loud melodies;—
And, as each God was born or had begun,
He in their order due and fit degrees
Sung of his birth and being—and did move
Apollo to unutterable love.

text:
It was an influence that Keats, with the unfolding of his own  
nature and genius, was rapidly outgrowing.
source_text:
It was an influence which Keats, with the development of his own
character and genius, was rapidly outgrowing.

text:
A nation proclaiming itself,
I alone bring forth the growth by which I may be known,
I spurn no one, welcome all, then remake all in my own shapes.
source_text:
A Nation announcing itself,
I myself make the only growth by which I can be appreciated,
I reject none, accept all, then reproduce all in my own forms.

text:
To the East and to the West,  
To the man of the Seaside State and of Pennsylvania,  
To the Canadian of the north, to the Southerner I love,  
With full and perfect trust I would render you as I render myself, the seeds are in all men,  
I believe the chief intent of these States is to build a noble  
friendship, exalted, hitherto unknown,  
Because I see it lies there, and has long been lying there, hidden in all men.
source_text:
To the East and to the West,
To the man of the Seaside State and of Pennsylvania,
To the Kanadian of the north, to the Southerner I love,
These with perfect trust to depict you as myself, the germs are in all men,
I believe the main purport of these States is to found a superb
friendship, exalte, previously unknown,
Because I perceive it waits, and has been always waiting, latent in all men.

text:
If I were certain, when this life was done,
That yours and mine would be,
I’d cast it off, like some discarded rind,
And savor eternity.
source_text:
If certain, when this life was out,
That yours and mine should be,
I'd toss it yonder like a rind,
And taste eternity.

text:
Yet soul, be certain the first intent endures, and will be fulfilled,
Perhaps, even now, the hour has come.
source_text:
Yet soul be sure the first intent remains, and shall be carried out,
Perhaps even now the time has arrived.

text:
A lofty casement, triple-arched, there stood,
All wreathed with carved devices in relief
Of fruit and bloom, and tufts of knotted grass,
And studded with panes of curious contrivance,
Countless in stain and rich in glowing hues,
Like the tiger-moth's deep damask'd pinions;
And in the centre, 'mid a thousand blazons,
And saints half-lost in twilight, and faint shields,
A guarded escutcheon glowed with queens' and kings' red blood.
source_text:
A casement high and triple-arch'd there was,
All garlanded with carven imag'ries
Of fruits, and flowers, and bunches of knot-grass,
And diamonded with panes of quaint device,
Innumerable of stains and splendid dyes,
As are the tiger-moth's deep-damask'd wings;
And in the midst, 'mong thousand heraldries,
And twilight saints, and dim emblazonings,
A shielded scutcheon blush'd with blood of queens and kings.

text:
At last arrived the night, ah! fearful hour,  
Ah! numbing time that stirs the dead,  
When demons ride the clouds in threatening power,  
—A phantom settled on my bed.
source_text:
At last came night, ah! horrid hour,
Ah! chilling time that wakes the dead,
When demons ride the clouds that lower,
—The phantom sat upon my bed.

text:
And old Silenus, brandishing a green wand
Of lilies, and the wood-gods, troop by troop,
Came, merry, as through olive thickets fanned
The cicadas come, made drunk by noonday’s drop:
And Dryope and Faunus hurried on,
Provoking the God to give them some new song;
Till in this cave they found the lady there,
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.
source_text:
And old Silenus, shaking a green stick
Of lilies, and the wood-gods in a crew
Came, blithe, as in the olive copses thick
Cicadae are, drunk with the noonday dew:
And Dryope and Faunus followed quick,
Teasing the God to sing them something new;
Till in this cave they found the lady lone,
Sitting upon a seat of emerald stone.

text:
My footfall frightens the wood-drake and wood-duck on my far and  
all-day wandering,
They rise as one, they wheel on slowly in a widening circle.
source_text:
My tread scares the wood-drake and wood-duck on my distant and
day-long ramble,
They rise together, they slowly circle around.

text: We hypothesize that, although language-model pre-training is theoretically orthogonal to MTL (Swayamdipta et al., 2018), in practice the gains from the two approaches tend to overlap. To disentangle these factors, we first employ the relatively weak GloVe representation to examine task relatedness, and then turn to BERT to show how much MTL can still improve performance when strong, contextualized representations are already available. As reported in Table 6 (GloVe), the tasks are not equally helpful to one another. In particular, (1) for OpenIE and ORL, multi-task learning with SRL yields substantial performance gains, whereas other tasks provide smaller or no improvements. (2) Dependency parsing and SRL serve as general source tasks that benefit most target tasks. This unified SpanRel framework facilitates MTL and makes it straightforward to identify useful source tasks.
source_text: We hypothesize that although language modeling pre-training is theoretically orthogonal to MTL (Swayamdipta et al., 2018) , in practice their benefits tends to overlap. To analyze these two factors separately, we start with a weak representation GloVe to study task relatedness, then move to BERT to demonstrate how much we can still improve with MTL given strong and contextualized representations. As shown in Table 6 (GloVe), tasks are not equally useful to each other. Notably, (1) for OpenIE and ORL, multi-task learning with SRL improves the performance significantly, while other tasks lead to less or no improvements. (2) Dependency parsing and SRL are generic source tasks that are beneficial to most of the target tasks. This unified SpanRel makes it easy to perform MTL and decide beneficial source tasks.

text: NER models frequently depend on convolutional or recurrent neural architectures, occasionally augmented with a CRF layer (Chiu and Nichols, 2016; Lample et al., 2016; Yadav and Bethard, 2018). More recently, deep contextualized representations based on bidirectional LSTMs (Peters et al., 2018), transformers (Devlin et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019), or contextual string embeddings (Akbik et al., 2019) have likewise been shown to attain state-of-the-art results on NER tasks.
source_text: NER models often rely on convolutional or recurrent neural architectures, sometimes completed by a CRF layer (Chiu and Nichols, 2016; Lample et al., 2016; Yadav and Bethard, 2018) . More recently, deep contextualised representations relying on bidirectional LSTMS (Peters et al., 2018) , transformers (Devlin et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019) or contextual string embeddings (Akbik et al., 2019) have also been shown to achieve state-of-the-art performance on NER tasks.

text: Reference-free MT evaluation. Recently, interest has been increasing in reference-free MT evaluation (Ma et al., 2019), also known as “quality estimation” (QE) within the MT community. In this setting, evaluation metrics perform a semantic comparison between system translations and the source sentences directly. The appeal of automatic reference-free MT evaluation is evident: it does not require human annotation or parallel data. To address this task, Popović et al. (2011) use a bag-of-word translation model to estimate translation quality by summing the likelihoods of aligned word pairs between source and translation texts. Specia et al. (2013) estimate translation quality with language-agnostic linguistic features extracted from source-language texts and system translations. Lo et al. (2014) propose XMEANT as a crosslingual, reference-free variant of MEANT, a metric grounded in semantic frames. Lo (2019) later extended this approach by incorporating M-BERT embeddings. The resulting metric, YiSi-2, assesses system translations by summing similarity scores over word pairs that are best-aligned mutual translations. YiSi-2-SRL optionally adds an extra similarity score based on alignments over semantic structures (e.g., semantic roles and frames). Although both metrics are reference-free, YiSi-2-SRL is not resource-lean because it requires a semantic parser for both languages. Furthermore, unlike our proposed metrics, they neither address misalignment in cross-lingual embedding spaces nor incorporate a target-side language model, which we consider crucial components.
source_text: Reference-free MT evaluation. Recently, there has been a growing interest in reference-free MT evaluation (Ma et al., 2019) , also referred to as "quality estimation" (QE) in the MT community. In this setup, evaluation metrics semantically compare system translations directly to the source sentences. The attractiveness of automatic referencefree MT evaluation is obvious: it does not require any human effort or parallel data. To approach this task, Popović et al. (2011) exploit a bag-ofword translation model to estimate translation quality, which sums over the likelihoods of aligned word-pairs between source and translation texts. Specia et al. (2013) estimate translation quality using language-agnostic linguistic features extracted from source lanuage texts and system translations. Lo et al. (2014) introduce XMEANT as a crosslingual reference-free variant of MEANT, a metric based on semantic frames. Lo (2019) extended this idea by leveraging M-BERT embeddings. The resulting metric, YiSi-2, evaluates system translations by summing similarity scores over words pairs that are best-aligned mutual translations. YiSi-2-SRL optionally combines an additional similarity score based on the alignment over the semantic structures (e.g., semantic roles and frames). Both metrics are reference-free, but YiSi-2-SRL is not resource-lean as it requires a semantic parser for both languages. Moreover, in contrast to our proposed metrics, they do not mitigate the misalignment of cross-lingual embedding spaces and do not integrate a target-side language model, which we identify to be crucial components.

text: In this work, we concentrate on the Search-based Pseudocode to Code (SPoC) dataset (Kulal et al., 2019) because it contains challenging multiline programs and provides input-output test suites for evaluating denotation accuracy. The dataset includes line-level pseudocode annotations for 18,356 C++ programs collected from crowd workers on Amazon Mechanical Turk. Following Kulal et al. (2019), we first generate candidate code fragments for each line using an off-the-shelf neural machine translation system. We then seek the highest-scoring combination of fragments that yields a valid program. Although identifying the optimal program in this setting is NP-hard once variable usage constraints are imposed (see Section A.3), it can be approximated by means of a hierarchical beam search. Our algorithm initially searches for semantic scaffolds for the program and then composes fragments conditioned on these scaffolds. This hierarchical strategy accelerates the search, yields higher-quality variants, and results in substantial gains in the final accuracy of our system.
source_text: In this work we focus on the Search-based Pseudocode to Code (SPoC) dataset (Kulal et al., 2019) due to its challenging multiline programs and availability of input-output test suites to evaluate denotation accuracy. The dataset contains line-level pseudocode annotations for 18,356 C++ programs provided by crowdsource workers from Amazon Mechanical Turk. As in the approach of Kulal et al. (2019) , we first obtain candidate code fragments for each line using an off-the-shelf neural machine translation system. We then aim to find the highestscoring combination of fragments that results in a valid program. Although finding the optimal program under this setting is NP-hard when variable usage constraints are introduced (see Section A.3), we can approximate it with a hierarchical beam search. Our algorithm first searches for semantic scaffolds for the program, then assembles fragments together conditioned on these scaffolds. This hierarchical approach speeds up search, produces higher quality variations, and leads to substantial improvements in our system's final accuracy.

text: To gain a clearer understanding of the various kinds of feedback supplied by our annotators, we randomly sample 200 examples from the dataset and label them according to feedback type. We begin by assigning each instance to one of three categories: (1) Complete: the feedback fully specifies how the predicted SQL should be corrected, (2) Partial: the feedback suggests a way to correct the predicted SQL, but only incompletely, and (3) Paraphrase: the feedback is a restatement of the original question. In the sample, 81.5% of the feedback was Complete, 13.5% was Partial, and 5.0% was Paraphrase. Examples of each feedback type are provided in Table 2. On closer examination of the Partial and Paraphrase cases, we find that they mostly arise when the distance between the predicted and gold SQL is large, i.e., in cases of major parsing errors. In such situations, annotators either choose to provide partial feedback that at least fixes some of the errors, or opt to rewrite the question in an alternative form.
source_text: To better understand the different types of feedback our annotators provided, we sample 200 examples from the dataset, and annotate them with the type of the feedback. We start by assigning the feedback to one of three categories: (1) Complete: the feedback fully describes how the predicted SQL can be corrected , (2) Partial: the feedback describes a way to correct the predicted SQL but only partially and (3) Paraphrase: the feedback is a paraphrase of the original question. The sample had 81.5% for Complete, 13.5% for Partial and 5.0% for Paraphrase feedback. Examples of each type of feedback are shown in Table 2 . Upon further inspection of the partial and paraphrase feedback, we observe that they mostly happen when the distance between the predicted and gold SQL is high (major parsing errors). As such, annotators opt for providing partial feedback (that would at least correct some of the mistakes) or decide to rewrite the question in a different way.

text: For each input collection A, we construct sparse TF-IDF unigram bag-of-words vectors for all articles in A. We then apply clustering algorithms to these vectors. For article clustering, we employ Markov Clustering (MCL) with a temporal constraint. MCL (Van Dongen, 2000) is a graph-based clustering method, that is, a community detection algorithm. It operates by simulating random walks over the nodes of a graph. Ribeiro et al. (2017) adopt this strategy for clustering news articles.
source_text: For each input collection A, we compute sparse TF-IDF unigram bag-of-words vectors for all articles in A. We apply clustering algorithms to these vectors. To cluster articles, we use Markov Clustering (MCL) with a temporal constraint. MCL (Van Dongen, 2000) is a clustering algorithm for graphs, i.e., a community detection algorithm. It is based on simulating random walks along nodes in a graph. Ribeiro et al. (2017) use this approach for clustering news articles.

text: Recent progress in NLP has been driven by a transition from static word embeddings to contextualized representations, such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and those derived from large transformer-based models like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Likewise, in text scoring, some studies have incorporated contextualized word embeddings to enhance performance (Nadeem et al., 2019). We extend these approaches to the grading of spoken transcriptions in settings where the audio, or any information derived from it, is unavailable. In other words, the task is similar to essay scoring, except that it includes characteristic features of speech such as false starts, repetitions, and filled pauses (Moore et al., 2015; Carter and McCarthy, 2017).
source_text: The field of NLP has seen advances recently thanks to a shift from fixed word embeddings to contextualized representations such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and those which can be obtained from large transformer models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) . Similarly in text scoring, some have incorporated contextualized word embeddings to improve performance (Nadeem et al., 2019) . We now apply such approaches to the grading of spoken transcriptions in a scenario where the audio, or information derived from it, is not available. In other words the task is analogous to essay scoring except for the presence of characteristic speech features such as false starts, repetitions and filled pauses (Moore et al., 2015; Carter and McCarthy, 2017) .

text: We train and evaluate our model on the PARANMT-50M paraphrase dataset, which was constructed by back-translating the Czech sentences in the CzEng corpus (Bojar et al., 2016). We further filter this dataset to exclude shorter sentences (fewer than 8 tokens), low-quality paraphrase pairs, as measured by a translation score provided with the dataset, and examples showing little reordering, as quantified by a reordering score based on the position of each word in the source sentence and its aligned word in the target sentence. After filtering, the dataset contains over 350k paired paraphrases.
source_text: We train and evaluate our model on the PARANMT-50M paraphrase dataset constructed by backtranslating the Czech sentences of the CzEng (Bojar et al., 2016) corpus. We filter this dataset to remove shorter sentences (less than 8 tokens), low quality paraphrase pairs (quantified by a translation score included with the dataset) and examples that exhibit low reordering (quantified by a reordering score based on the position of each word in the source and its aligned word in the target sentence). This leaves us with over 350k paired paraphrase pairs.

text: On ISNotes, Hou (2018a) previously reported the best performance by incorporating the prediction of a deterministic algorithm, namely embeddings bridging (NP head + modifiers), as an additional feature in the global inference model (MLN II) proposed by Hou et al. (2013b). This deterministic algorithm relies on word embeddings for bridging and represents the meaning of an NP on the basis of its head noun and modifiers.
source_text: On ISNotes, previously Hou (2018a) reported the best result by adding the prediction from a deterministic algorithm (embeddings bridging (NP head + modifiers)) as an additional feature into the global inference model (MLN II) proposed by Hou et al. (2013b) . The deterministic algorithm is based on word embeddings for bridging and models the meaning of an NP based on its head noun and modifications.

text: Compared with B4 (S2sa-at-mp-gsa), the gains are more pronounced in terms of “Fluency” (+13.33%) and “Complexity” (+8.48%) than in “Relevance” (+6.27%). This is because the baseline generates questions that are more superficial, thereby lowering complexity, or questions containing semantic mistakes, thereby reducing fluency. Similar patterns are observed when the semantic graph is removed (A2.w/o Semantic Graph). These findings indicate that, by incorporating the semantic graph, our model generates questions with fewer semantic errors and makes better use of contextual information.
source_text: • Compared against B4 (S2sa-at-mp-gsa), improvements are more salient in terms of "Fluency" (+13.33%) and "Complexity" (+8.48%) than that of "Relevance" (+6.27%). The reason is that the baseline produces more shallow questions (affecting complexity) or questions with semantic errors (affecting fluency). We observe similar results when removing the semantic graph (A2.w/o Semantic Graph). These demonstrate that our model, by incorporating the semantic graph, produces questions with fewer semantic errors and utilizes more context.

text: Does training on paragraph- or sentence-level representations of a premise offer any benefit? The following experiments evaluate BERT-class models and SVMs trained with the paragraph (Para) and sentence (WMD-n) representations. The outcomes of these experiments are reported in Table 6. Results and Analysis: We observe that training on the INFOTABS training set yields a substantial improvement in model performance over the earlier baselines, with the exception of the straightforward SVM model that depends on unigrams and bigrams. RoBERTa L exceeds both its base version and BERT B by approximately ∼ 9% and ∼ 14%, respectively. Consistent with the earlier finding, using the complete premise performs better than choosing only a subset of sentences. Crucially, performance on α 2 and α 3 is worse than on α 1 , which not only indicates the greater difficulty of these data splits, but also suggests that the models overfit either lexical regularities (as reflected by α 2 ) or domain-specific regularities (as reflected by α 3 ).
source_text: Does training on the paragraph/sentence representation of a premise help? The next set of experiments compares BERT-class models and SVM trained using the paragraph (Para) and sentence (WMD-n) representations. The results for these experiments are presented in Table 6 . Results and Analysis: We find that training with the INFOTABS training set improves model performance significantly over the previous baselines, except for the simple SVM model which relies on unigrams and bigrams. We see that RoBERTa L outperforms its base variant and BERT B by around ∼ 9% and ∼ 14% respectively. Similar to the earlier observation, providing full premise is better than selecting a subset of sentences. Importantly, α 2 and α 3 performance is worse than α 1 , not only suggesting the difficulty of these data splits, but also showing that models overfit both lexical patterns (based on α 2 ) or domainspecific patterns (based on α 3 ).

text: We perform experiments on the WebNLG dataset (Gardent et al., 2017; Castro Ferreira et al., 2018) employed in the WebNLG challenge. 5 For each example, the input consists of a set of up to seven RDF triples from DBPedia, and the target is a textual description of those triples. Each triple set is associated with up to three human-written reference texts. In addition, each reference is annotated with the order in which the triples were realized, and we use these annotations to train and evaluate our Planner. In total, the dataset comprises 9,674 distinct triple sets and 25,298 text references, and it is split into training, development, and test sets. The test set contains two subsets: the SEEN portion, in which the instances come from one of the nine domains observed in the training and development sets (e.g., Astronaut and Food), and the UNSEEN portion, in which the instances are drawn from the other five unseen domains. The UNSEEN portion is intended to assess models’ ability to generalize to out-of-domain instances.
source_text: We conduct experiments on the WebNLG dataset (Gardent et al., 2017; Castro Ferreira et al., 2018) used in the WebNLG challenge. 5 For each instance, the input is a set of up to 7 RDF triples from DBPedia, and the output is their text descriptions. Each triple-set is paired with a set of (up to three) humangenerated reference texts. Each reference is also paired with the order of triples it realized. We use them to train and evaluate our Planner. Overall, the dataset contains 9, 674 unique triple-sets and 25, 298 text references, and is divided into training, development, and test set. The test set contains two subsets, the SEEN part where the instances belong to one of the nine domains that are seen in the training and development set (such as Astronaut and Food), and the UNSEEN part where the instances are from the other five unseen domains. The UNSEEN part is designed to evaluate models' generalizability to out-of-domain instances.

This model has four convolution layers; at each layer the intermediate representation u i is computed by a max-pooling operation over feature maps. text: Decoding Strategies Thus far, we have only evaluated perplexity, whereas our actual objective is generation, which necessitates decoding. We examine several standard approaches: greedy decoding; beam search, with beam size and minimum and maximum output length as hyperparameters; beam search with blocking, which prevents repeated n-grams, where we use n = 3 (Paulus et al., 2018); and nucleus sampling, parameterized by p (Holtzman et al., 2019). The impact of these choices is reported in Table 6 for ConvAI2 and Wizard of Wikipedia (WoW).
source_text: Decoding Strategies So far, we have only been measuring perplexity, but we are actually interested in generation, which requires us to decode. We consider several standard approaches: greedy, beam search (with beam size, and minimum and maximum output length 2 hyperparameters), beam search with beam blocking (blocking n-grams, we use n = 3) (Paulus et al., 2018) and nucleus sampling (with parameter p) (Holtzman et al., 2019) . We show the effect of these choices in Table 6 for ConvAI2 and Wizard of Wikipedia (WoW).

text: Recent promising conversational models have drawn on very large conversation-style datasets, including corpora mined from Reddit and distributed by a third party via pushshift.io (Mazaré et al., 2018; Humeau et al., 2019; Keskar et al., 2019; Rashkin et al., 2019). Such large-scale resources are highly valuable because they supply abundant conversational data, enabling reproducible research and direct comparison with prior work; however, the characteristics of the resulting dialogue agents depend on those present in the source conversations. Since online exchanges can become toxic and may be lacking in empathy, indiscriminate pretraining on these corpora is unlikely to automatically impart desirable properties to a conversational agent, such as avoiding toxic outputs (Dinan et al., 2019a), exhibiting empathy (Rashkin et al., 2019), or providing knowledge (Dinan et al., 2019b).
source_text: Recent promising conversational models have leveraged very large conversation-like data such as datasets extracted from Reddit and made available by a third party on pushshift.io (Mazaré et al., 2018; Humeau et al., 2019; Keskar et al., 2019; Rashkin et al., 2019) . These large-scale datasets are very useful in providing vast amounts of conversational material that allow for reproducible research and comparison with prior work, however the qualities of resulting conversational agents are dependent on the qualities present in the source conversations. Given how online conversations can turn toxic and lack empathy, indiscriminate pretraining on such corpora is unlikely to spontaneously endow a conversational agent with desirable qualities such as avoiding toxic responses (Dinan et al., 2019a) or demonstrating empathy (Rashkin et al., 2019) or knowledge (Dinan et al., 2019b) .

text: Our second study investigated the veridicality of the generated summaries, that is, whether the facts they mention are actually discussed in the input reviews. Participants were presented with the reviews together with the corresponding summary and were asked to judge, for each summary sentence, whether it was fully supported by the reviews, partially supported, or not supported at all. We conducted this experiment only on Yelp, because the number of reviews is small and participants could read them all in a reasonable amount of time. We used the same 50 instances as in our first study and obtained five judgments per instance. Participants evaluated summaries generated by MEANSUM and DENOISESUM. We also included GOLD-standard summaries as an upper bound, but did not include any output from an extractive system, since such output by default contains facts mentioned in the reviews. Table 5 reports the percentages of fully supported (Full-Supp), partially supported (PartSupp), and unsupported (No-Supp) sentences. Gold summaries achieve the highest proportion of fully supported sentences (63.3%), followed by DENOISESUM (55.1%) and MEANSUM (41.7%). These findings are encouraging, suggesting that our model hallucinates less than MEANSUM.
source_text: Our second study examined the veridicality of the generated summaries, namely whether the facts mentioned in them are indeed discussed in the input reviews. Participants were shown reviews and the corresponding summary and were asked to verify for each summary sentence whether it was fully supported by the reviews, partially supported, or not at all supported. We performed this experiment on Yelp only since the number of reviews is small and participants could read them all in a timely fashion. We used the same 50 instances as in our first study and collected five judgments per instance. Participants assessed the summaries produced by MEANSUM and DENOISESUM. We also included GOLD-standard summaries as an upper bound but no output from an extractive system as it by default contains facts mentioned in the reviews. Table 5 reports the percentage of fully (Full-Supp), partially (PartSupp), and un-supported (No-Supp) sentences. Gold summaries display the highest percentage of fully supported sentences (63.3%), followed by DENOISESUM (55.1%), and MEANSUM (41.7%). These results are encouraging, indicating that our model hallucinates to a lesser extent compared to MEANSUM.

text: Understanding how people read and process text has long been a major topic in both cognitive science and natural language processing (NLP) research (Graesser et al., 1980; Liversedge et al., 1998; Frank et al., 2013a; Busjahn et al., 2014; Seppi, 2019, 2020). Online content providers and consumers are likewise interested in this line of work; in today’s increasingly busy world, readers often do not have time for lengthy articles, which leads content creators to target particular reading durations. Many providers have even analyzed traffic patterns to identify the ideal content length, with the general consensus suggesting that 3–7 minutes of work done as part of a capstone course with Adobe 1 Medium's study can be found here. content is optimal. Having determined the preferred content length, article writers then face the next challenge: when has a post reached that ideal length? A news article about last night’s football game may be easier to read than a technical post about NLP. Likewise, font type or size may affect a consumer’s comprehension and thereby slow the reading process. Many factors, both textual and stylistic, therefore immediately come to mind when considering the possible reading time of an article.
source_text: Understanding how we read and process text has proven a large area of both cognitive science and natural language processing (NLP) research (Graesser et al., 1980; Liversedge et al., 1998; Frank et al., 2013a; Busjahn et al., 2014; Seppi, 2019, 2020) . Online content providers and consumers are also interested in this research; in the increasingly busy world of today, consumers lack the time to read long articles, prompting content creators to aim for specific reading lengths. Many providers 1 have even examined traffic patterns in order to determine the ideal content length, with the general consensus finding 3-7 minutes of Work done as part of a capstone course with Adobe 1 Medium's study can be found here. content optimal. Thus, having established the optimal content length, article writers now face the next hurdle: when has their post reached the ideal length? A news article about last night's football game may be easier to read than a technical post about NLP. Perhaps the font type or size influences the consumer's comprehension, slowing down the reading process. There are many factors, both textual and stylistic, that quickly come to mind when considering the potential reading time of an article.

text: For many NLP applications, the most widely used transfer learning approaches begin by pre-training a large language model, such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018), GPT (Radford et al., 2019), and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Such a language model is able to encode broad syntactic and semantic information that can then be leveraged in downstream NLP tasks. It is especially appealing because it can be trained entirely in an unsupervised way using very large quantities of unlabeled data, which are now extremely inexpensive to obtain from the internet. The resulting massively large multidomain text corpus makes it possible to train very large language models. To the best of our knowledge, the largest language model to date is T5, with an enormous scale of roughly 11 billion parameters (Raffel et al., 2019).
source_text: For many applications in NLP, most popular transfer learning methods choose to pre-train a large language model, e.g., ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) , GPT (Radford et al., 2019) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) . Such a language model can capture general semantic and syntactic information that can be further used in downstream NLP tasks. The language model is particularly attractive, because it can be trained in a completely unsupervised manner with huge amount of unlabeled data, which are extremely cheap to fetch from internet nowadays. The resulting extremely large multidomain text corpus allows us to train huge language models. To the best of our knowledge, by far the largest language model, T5, has an enormous size of about 11 billion parameters (Raffel et al., 2019) .

text: The ATIS (Airline Travel Information System) dataset (Hakkani-Tür et al., 2010) is widely used to assess the NLU component. Here, we concentrate on more intricate dialogue phenomena that arise in real-world settings but are not represented in ATIS or in other alternatives such as DSTC (Henderson et al., 2014) or SNIPS 1. For instance, consider a plausible user utterance, “can i get two medium veggie pizza and one small lemonade” (Figure 2A). The intent is OrderItems. Two items are mentioned, and each item has three properties: the item name (veggie pizza, lemonade), the item quantity (two, one), and the item size (medium, small). To satisfy the customer’s request, these properties must be correctly associated with one another—the customer would not be satisfied with one small veggie pizza. This type of structure appears only to a limited degree in the ATIS dataset (Figure 2B), which includes particular forms such as from loc.city name and to loc.city name that must be distinguished. Nevertheless, the dataset is small enough that these may be treated as separate labels, and multi-class slot-labeling methods that predict each specific form as an independent class (Figure 1) have been successful. In more open domains, however, this conversion from hierarchy to multi-class labels causes the number of classes to grow exponentially compared with an approach that properly exploits the available structure. Moreover, hierarchical relations, for example between fromloc and city name, are disregarded, which restricts the sharing of data and statistical power across labels.
source_text: The ATIS (Airline Travel Information System) dataset (Hakkani-Tür et al., 2010) is widely used for evaluating the NLU component. We focus on complex aspects of dialog that occur in real-world scenarios but are not captured in ATIS or other alternatives such as, DSTC (Henderson et al., 2014) or SNIPS 1 . As an example, consider a reasonable user utterance, "can i get two medium veggie pizza and one small lemonade" (Figure 2A ). The intent is OrderItems. There are two items mentioned, each with three properties. The properties are the name of the item (veggie pizza, lemonade), the quantity of the item (two, one) and size of the item (medium, small). These properties need to be grouped together accurately to successfully fulfill the customer's request -the customer would not be happy with one small veggie pizza. This structure occurs to a limited extent in the ATIS dataset ( Figure 2B ), which has specific forms such as, from loc.city name and to loc.city name, which must be distinguished. However, the scale is small enough that these can be separate labels and multi-class slot-labeling approaches that predict each specific form as a separate class ( Figure 1 ) have had success. In more open domains, this hierarchy-to-multi-class conversion increases the number of classes exponentially vs. an approach that appropriately uses available structure. Further, hierarchical relationships, e.g. between fromloc and city name, are ignored, which limits the sharing of data and statistical strength across labels.

text: Model 2: Phone End-to-End. Our final model employs phone-factored embeddings, in which trainable embeddings of phone features are concatenated with the standard speech feature-vector input. Since phone durations vary and typically extend over more than one filterbank feature, or frame, adjacent filterbank features may share the same predicted phone label; in the example in Figure 1, /R/ covers three frames, or filterbank features. We note that this approach preserves the original speech-feature sequence length. It links similar feature vectors at the corpus level, because all filterbank features with the same phone alignment (e.g. /OH/) receive the same concatenated trainable phone embedding. In MT and NER, concatenating trainable embeddings of linguistic features to words, such as morphemes and phones, has been shown to improve models’ ability to generalize (Sennrich and Haddow, 2016; Chaudhary et al., 2018). Whereas those studies appended finer-grained information to connect words with similar lower-level structure, we use phone embeddings to connect higher-level structure to similar but distinct speech feature vectors globally across a corpus.
source_text: Model 2: Phone End-to-End. Our final model uses phone-factored embeddings, where trainable embeddings for phone features are concatenated to typical speech feature vector input. Because phone durations are variable and typically span more than one filterbank feature (or frame), adjacent filterbank features may have the predicted phone label; in the example shown in Figure 1, /R/ spans three frames or filterbank features. We note that this method maintains the same source sequence length as the original speech feature sequence. This method associates similar feature vectors at the corpus level, because all filterbank features with the same phone alignment (e.g. /OH/) will have the same trainable phone embedding concatenated. In MT and NER, concatenating trainable embeddings for linguistic features to words, such as morphemes and phones, has improved models' ability to generalize (Sennrich and Haddow, 2016; Chaudhary et al., 2018) . While these works appended finer-grained information to associate words with similar lower-level structure, we use phone embeddings to associate higher-level structure to similar but unique speech feature vectors globally across a corpus.

text: (BPE) (Sennrich et al., 2016) is used to tokenize both the input and output sentences. These models are trained in the standard manner by maximizing the log likelihood of the target sequence with teacher forcing. In addition, to ensure that the decoder does not repeatedly attend to the same input tokens at each decoding step, we incorporate a coverage loss term, following See et al. (2017). Note that because the transformer architecture is non-recurrent, it adds position embeddings to the input word embeddings to indicate the correct sequence of words in both x and y (see Figure 2). In this work, we propose an additional set of position embeddings to specify the intended word order during generation, as described next.
source_text: (BPE) (Sennrich et al., 2016) to tokenize our input and output sentences. These models are trained in the standard way, maximizing the log likelihood of the target sequence using teacher forcing. Additionally, in order to ensure that the decoder does not attend to the same input tokens repeatedly at each step of the decoding process, we include a coverage loss term, as proposed in See et al. (2017) . Note that since the architecture of the transformer model is non-recurrent, it adds position embeddings to the input word embeddings in order to indicate the correct sequence of the words in both x and y (see Figure 2 ). In this work, we propose using an additional set of position embeddings to indicate the desired order of words during generation, described next.

text: The objectives outlined in Sections 3.1 and 3.2 require us to align an approximate posterior, either q(z|x) or q(z|y), with a prior p(z) that encodes our prior belief. A widely used choice for p(z) in the literature is the Normal distribution (Kingma and Welling, 2013). However, it is important to note that even if both q(z|x) and q(z|y) are matched to the same prior, this does not ensure that the two inferred posteriors will be close to one another; such closeness is a desirable characteristic of a shared latent space. To more directly promote this property, we propose a novel prior selection strategy: when the posterior is inferred from x (i.e., q(z|x)), we use the parameterized distribution q(z|y) as our prior belief p(z). Likewise, when the posterior is inferred from y (i.e., q(z|y)), we are free to define p(z) as q(z|x). This strategy explicitly brings q(z|x) and q(z|y) closer together, thereby encouraging a shared latent space.
source_text: The objectives described in 3.1 and 3.2 require us to match an approximated posterior (either q(z|x) or q(z|y)) to a prior p(z) that reflects our belief. A common choice of p(z) in the research literature is the Normal distribution (Kingma and Welling, 2013). However, it should be noted that even if we match both q(z|x) and q(z|y) to the same prior, it does not guarantee that the two inferred posteriors are close to each other; this is a desired property of the shared latent space. To better address the property, we propose a novel prior choice: when the posterior is inferred from x (i.e., q(z|x)), we choose the parameterised distribution q(z|y) as our prior belief of p(z). Similarly, when the posterior is inferred from y (i.e., q(z|y)), we have the freedom of defining p(z) to be q(z|x). This approach directly pulls q(z|x) and q(z|y) closer to ensure a shared latent space.

text: Our contributions may be summarized as follows: (a) we develop methods for infusing knowledge of narrative structure into generic supervised and unsupervised summarization algorithms; (b) we introduce a new layer of annotations for the CSI corpus, which can support research on long-form summarization; and (c) we show that narrative structure can aid screenplay summarization; our analysis indicates that key events identified in the latent space correlate with important summary content.
source_text: Our contributions can be summarized as follows: (a) we develop methods for instilling knowledge about narrative structure into generic su-pervised and unsupervised summarization algorithms; (b) we provide a new layer of annotations for the CSI corpus, which can be used for research in long-form summarization; and (c) we demonstrate that narrative structure can facilitate screenplay summarization; our analysis shows that key events identified in the latent space correlate with important summary content.

text: Text generation is a significant area of research in machine learning. Recent studies have demonstrated strong performance on various tasks by integrating reinforcement learning (RL) with generative models. Representative applications include image captioning (Ren et al., 2017; Rennie et al., 2016), text summarization (Li et al., 2018b; Paulus et al., 2017; Rush et al., 2015), and adversarial text generation (Guo et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2018). The sequence-to-sequence framework (Seq2Seq) (Sutskever et al., 2014) is a widely used approach for text generation. However, models trained in this setting are generally optimized to predict the next token from preceding ground-truth tokens as input, which leads to the so-called exposure bias (Ranzato et al., 2016). In contrast, sequence-level training with RL offers an effective way to address this issue by formulating text generation as a sequential decision-making problem. By directly optimizing an evaluation score (cumulative rewards) (Ranzato et al., 2016), state-of-the-art results have been achieved on many text-generation tasks (Paulus et al., 2017; Rennie et al., 2016). Nevertheless, a difficulty in this framework is that rewards during RL training are especially sparse, because a scalar reward is usually obtained only after the full sequence has been generated. In addition, recurrent models emphasize local fluency more strongly and may result in inconsistent semantic meanings in long text generation.
source_text: Text generation is an important area of investigation within machine learning. Recent work has shown excellent performance on a number of tasks, by combining reinforcement learning (RL) and generative models. Example applications include image captioning (Ren et al., 2017; Rennie et al., 2016 ), text summarization (Li et al., 2018b; Paulus et al., 2017; Rush et al., 2015) , and adversarial text generation (Guo et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2018) . The sequence-to-sequence framework (Seq2Seq) (Sutskever et al., 2014 ) is a popular technique for text generation. However, models from such a setup are typically trained to predict the next token given previous ground-truth tokens as input, causing what is termed exposure bias (Ranzato et al., 2016) . By contrast, sequence-level training with RL provides an effective means of solving this challenge, by treating text generation as a sequential decision-making problem. By directly optimizing an evaluation score (cumulative rewards) (Ranzato et al., 2016) , state-of-the-art results have been ob-tained in many text-generation tasks (Paulus et al., 2017; Rennie et al., 2016) . However, one problem in such a framework is that rewards in RL training are particularly sparse, since a scalar reward is typically only available after an entire sequence has been generated. Furthermore, the recurrent models focus more on local fluency, and may cause inconsistent semantic meanings for long text generation.

text: Our approach comprises two components. First, we construct a synthetic dataset D = {(X, y)} of summary–review pairs. Concretely, we sample a review x_i from C, treat it as if it were a summary, and then produce several noisy variants of it, i.e., pseudoreviews. During training, a denoising model is learned to strip away the noise in these reviews and generate the corresponding summary. At test time, the same denoising model is applied to summarize real reviews. We employ denoising as an auxiliary task for opinion summarization to model the observation that summaries typically exclude opinions that do not reflect consensus, that is, noise in the pseudoreview, while retaining salient opinions that appear in most reviews, that is, the nonnoisy portions of the pseudoreview.
source_text: Our method consists of two parts. We first create a synthetic dataset D = {(X, y)} consisting of summary-review pairs. Specifically, we sample review x i from C, pretend it is a summary, and generate multiple noisy versions thereof (i.e., pseudoreviews). At training time, a denoising model learns to remove the noise from the reviews and generate the summary. At test time, the same denoising model is used to summarize actual reviews. We use denoising as an auxiliary task for opinion summarization to simulate the fact that summaries tend to omit opinions that do not represent consensus (i.e., noise in the pseudo-review), but include salient opinions found in most reviews (i.e., nonnoisy parts of the pseudo-review).

text: We perform experiments on image captioning (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) to examine the gains introduced by the Guider network. In the image captioning task, rather than employing a discriminator to specify the final rewards for the generated sentence, we use evaluation metrics computed from human references. These final rewards are seemingly more influential, since they incorporate reference, i.e., ground-truth, information. The feature-matching rewards serve as a regularizer for the final rewards. In this setting, we refer to our model as a guider-matching sequence training (GMST) model. An overview of GMST is given in the Appendix. We evaluate the proposed model on the MS COCO dataset (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015), which contains 123,287 images in total. Each image is annotated with at least five captions. Following Karpathy's split (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015), 5,000 images are used for validation and another 5,000 for testing. We report BLEU-k scores (k ranging from 1 to 4), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015), and ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005). We consider two configurations: (i) using a pre-trained 152-layer ResNet (He et al., 2016) for feature extraction, where we take the output of the 2048-dimensional pool5 layer from ResNet-152 pretrained on ImageNet; and (ii) using semantic tags detected from the image as features. We use an LSTM with 512 hidden units and mini-batches of size 64. For optimization, we employ Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) with a learning rate of 2 × 10−4. We first pretrain the captioning model for up to 20 epochs, then train it with reinforcement learning for 20 additional epochs, and finally test the best model on the validation set.
source_text: We conduct experiments on image captioning (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , investigating benefits brought by the Guider network. In image captioning, instead of using a discriminator to define final rewards for generated sentence, we adopt evaluation metrics computed based on human references. The final rewards appear more important as they contain reference (ground-truth) information. Feature-matching rewards work as a regularizer of the final rewards. We call our model in this setting a guider-matching sequence training (GMST) model. An overview of GMST is provided in the Appendix. We test our proposed model on the MS COCO dataset (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , containing 123,287 images in total. Each image is annotated with at least 5 captions. Following Karpathy's split (Karpathy and Fei-Fei, 2015) , 5,000 images are used for both validation and testing. We report BLEU-k (k from 1 to 4), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015) , and ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) scores. We consider two settings: (i) using a pre-trained 152layer ResNet (He et al., 2016) for feature extraction, where we take the output of the 2048-way pool5 layer from ResNet-152, pretrained on the ImageNet dataset; and (ii) using semantic tags detected from the image as features . We use an LSTM with 512 hidden units with mini-batches of size 64. Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) is used for optimization, with learning rate 2 × 10 −4 . We pretrain the captioning model for the maximum 20 epochs, then use the reinforcement learning to train it for 20 epochs and test on the best model on the validation set.

text: Learning-to-rank formulations for answer selection in QA systems are standard practice, and they most often depend on pointwise ranking models (Severyn and Moschitti, 2015; Garg et al., 2019). Our use of discriminative re-ranking (Collins and Koo, 2005) with softmax loss is more akin to pairwise ranking, in which the multiclass margin between correct and incorrect answers is maximized (Joachims, 2002; Burges et al., 2005; Köppel et al., 2019). This represents an important difference from TREC-style answer selection, since our ST-generated candidate responses exhibit lower semantic, syntactic, and lexical variance, which makes pointwise methods less effective.
source_text: Learning to Rank formulations for answer selection in QA systems is common practice, most frequently relying on pointwise ranking models (Severyn and Moschitti, 2015; Garg et al., 2019) . Our use of discriminative re-ranking (Collins and Koo, 2005) with softmax loss is closer to learning a pairwise ranking by maximizing the multiclass margin between correct and incorrect answers (Joachims, 2002; Burges et al., 2005; Köppel et al., 2019) . This is an important distinction from TREC-style answer selection as our ST-generated candidate responses have lower semantic, syntactic, and lexical variance, making pointwise methods less effective.

text: Natural language explanations can encode different forms of inductive bias and prior knowledge, although some kinds of prior knowledge are naturally better incorporated through alternative mechanisms. We conclude our experiments with a vignette illustrating how language explanations can complement other approaches to feature and representation engineering. We focus on Disease, for which we have access to an external ontology, the Comparative Toxicogenomic Database (CTD) from Wei et al. (2015), containing chemical–disease interactions. Following Hancock et al. (2018), we append 6 bits to the explanation representation v(x) to indicate whether the provided chemical–disease pair satisfies specific relations in CTD (for example, whether it appears in the ctd-therapy dictionary). Table 5 shows that, as anticipated, additional information sources can complement language explanations in ExpBERT.
source_text: Natural language explanations can capture different types of inductive biases and prior knowledge, but some types of prior knowledge are of course better introduced through other means. We wrap up our experiments with a vignette on how language explanations can complement other forms of feature and representation engineering. We consider Disease, where we have access to an external ontology (Comparative Toxicogenomic Database or CTD) from Wei et al. (2015) containing chemicaldisease interactions. Following Hancock et al. (2018), we add 6 bits to the explanation representation v(x) that test if the given chemical-disease pair follows certain relations in CTD (e.g., if they are in the ctd-therapy dictionary). Table 5 shows that as expected, other sources of information can complement language explanations in ExpBERT.

text: As in prior academic studies on this corpus (Bansal et al., 2018), we employ a sequence-to-sequence architecture inspired by Weiss et al. (2017), adapted to operate under lower-resource conditions; in particular, each model reaches convergence in approximately 5 days on a single GPU. We implement encoder-decoder models with attention in xnmt using 512 hidden units. Our pyramidal encoder consists of 3-layer BiLSTMs with linear network-in-network (NiN) projections and batch normalization between layers (Zhang et al., 2017). These NiN projections downsample by a factor of 2 between layers, yielding the same overall 4× temporal downsampling as the extra convolutional layers in Weiss et al. 2017; Bansal et al. 2019. This provides the advantage of increased depth with fewer additional parameters. We use single-layer MLP attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015) with 128 units and a single decoder layer, rather than the 3 or 4 layers used in previous work, because we did not observe consistent gains from greater depth.
source_text: As in previous academic work on this corpus (Bansal et al., 2018; , we use a sequence-to-sequence architecture inspired by Weiss et al. (2017) modified to train within lower resources; specifically, each model converges within ≈5 days on one GPU. We build encoder-decoder models with attention in xnmt with 512 hidden units. Our pyramidal encoder uses 3-layer BiLSTMs with linear network-in-network (NiN) projections and batch normalization between layers Zhang et al., 2017) . The NiN projections are used to downsample by a factor of 2 between layers, resulting in the same total 4× downsampling in time as the additional convolutional layers from Weiss et al. 2017; Bansal et al. 2019: They give us the benefit of added depth with fewer additional parameters. We use single layer MLP attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015) with 128 units and 1 decoder layer as opposed to 3 or 4 in previous workwe did not see consistent benefits from additional depth.

text: From a different perspective, research in audio recognition has primarily focused on detecting simple single-word commands or keyword spotting (de Andrade et al., 2018), identifying acoustic events such as environmental or urban sounds (Salamon et al., 2014; Piczak, 2015; Xu et al., 2016), or music patterns, as well as document-level classification of complete audio sequences (Liu et al., 2017). McMahan and Rao (2018) offer a more comprehensive overview. Although methods in this area are related to ours—for example, in their use of convolutional networks over audio (Sainath and Parada, 2015; Salamon and Bello, 2017)—our task involves questions as linguistic units of substantially greater complexity.
source_text: From another viewpoint, work in audio recognition mainly involves with detecting simple singleword commands or keyword spotting (de Andrade et al., 2018) , recognizing acoustic events such as environmental or urban sounds (Salamon et al., 2014; Piczak, 2015; Xu et al., 2016) or music patterns, or document-level classification of entire audio sequences (Liu et al., 2017) . McMahan and Rao (2018) provide a more extensive overview. While approaches in this line of work relate to ours, e.g. in the use of convolutional networks over audio (Sainath and Parada, 2015; Salamon and Bello, 2017) , our challenge features questions as linguistic units of significantly greater complexity.
