text: Training Data To train our model, we use a subset of the Semantic Scholar corpus comprising approximately 146K query papers (about 26.7M tokens) together with their corresponding outgoing citations, and we reserve an additional 32K papers for validation. For each query paper, we create up to 5 training triples, each consisting of a query, a positive, and a negative paper. Positive papers are sampled from the query’s direct citations, whereas negative papers are selected either at random or from citations of citations (as described in §2.4). Empirically, we found it beneficial to use 2 hard negatives (citations of citations) and 3 easy negatives (randomly selected papers) for each query paper. This yields roughly 684K training triples and 145K validation triples.
source_text: Training Data To train our model, we use a subset of the Semantic Scholar corpus consisting of about 146K query papers (around 26.7M tokens) with their corresponding outgoing citations, and we use an additional 32K papers for validation. For each query paper we construct up to 5 training triples comprised of a query, a positive, and a negative paper. The positive papers are sampled from the direct citations of the query, while negative papers are chosen either randomly or from citations of citations (as discussed in §2.4). We empirically found it helpful to use 2 hard negatives (citations of citations) and 3 easy negatives (randomly selected papers) for each query paper. This process results in about 684K training triples and 145K validation triples.

text: We employ AllenNLP’s wrapper around HuggingFace’s Transformers code 1 for our implementation 2. For general-domain datasets, we initialize models with BERT-base-cased weights, and for clinical datasets we use bio-BERT weights (Lee et al., 2019) as initialization. Our analysis uses cased models, since bio-BERT (Lee et al., 2019) is itself cased. A single learning rate of 2e-5 was used across all experiments. We relied on validation splits supplied by the datasets. When no validation set was provided, as in MADE 1.0, EMRQA, or SQuAD1.1, we reserved 10% of the training data for validation. We set early stopping patience to 5, with each epoch comprising 20,000 steps. Rather than negative log likelihood (NLL), we monitored the final evaluation metric for training and early stopping. This was done to lessen the mis-calibration of the underlying p θ model, given Guo et al. (2017)’s observation that neural networks overfit on NLL. The implementation details for each experiment are given in the following subsections.
source_text: We use AllenNLP's wrapper with HuggingFace's Transformers code 1 for our implementation 2 . We use BERT-base-cased weights as the initialization for general-domain datasets and bio-BERT weights (Lee et al., 2019) as the initialization for clinical datasets. We use cased models for our analysis, since bio-BERT (Lee et al., 2019) uses cased models. A common learning rate of 2e-5 was used for all experiments. We used validation data splits provided by the datasets. In cases where the validation dataset was not provided, such as MADE 1.0, EMRQA or SQuAD1.1, we use 10% of the training data as the validation data. We use a patience of 5 for early stopping the model, with each epoch consisting of 20,000 steps. We use the final evaluation metric instead of negative log likelihood (NLL) to monitor and early stop the training. This is to reduce the mis-calibration of the underlying p θ model, since Guo et al. (2017) observe that neural nets overfit on NLL. The implementation for each experiment is provided in the following subsections.

text: Context and significance. Learning to assign labels to text sequences is among the most extensively studied problems in natural language processing. More recently, neural networks have been used not only for sequential labeling tasks such as part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) and named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016), but also to reformulate inherently non-sequential tasks, such as syntactic parsing, within a labeling framework (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019).
source_text: Context and relevance. Learning to label sequences of text is one of the more thoroughly explored topics in natural language processing. In recent times, neural networks are applied not only to sequential labeling like part-of-speech tagging (Plank et al., 2016) or named entity recognition (Ma and Hovy, 2016) , but also to cast into a labeling framework otherwise non-sequential tasks such as syntactic parsing (Gómez-Rodríguez and Vilares, 2018; Strzyz et al., 2019) .

text: In our corpora, each orthographic word corresponds to a unique correct segment sequence. For convenience, we therefore identify \(s_{1:n}\) with its associated orthographic form. By abuse of notation, we write \(p(\cdot \mid \cdot)\) for the distribution over segmental forms induced by the language model. The listener receives a segment sequence \(s_{1:n} = (s_1, \ldots, s_n)\)—for example, [SIg@ôEt] (“shigarette”)—drawn from a channel distribution conditioned on the speaker’s intended segment sequence: \(s_{1:n} \sim p(\cdot \mid s_{1:n})\). This captures the effect of noise on the signal received by the listener.

text: Limiting Behavior Another important observation is that importance-sampled estimates of perplexity are consistent, that is, they converge as the number of samples tends to infinity. To establish this, we first note that p(x) is consistent, a standard consequence of the strong law of large numbers (Geweke, 1989). Consequently, log p(x) is also consistent by the continuous mapping theorem (Van der Vaart, 2000).

text: Despite these limitations, we hope that LEAQI offers a useful, and relatively simple, bridge for incorporating rule-based systems, heuristics, and unsupervised models as components of more complex supervised learning systems. This is especially appealing in settings where very strong rule-based systems are available, since such systems often surpass the best statistical systems, as in coreference resolution (Lee et al., 2011), information extraction (Riloff and Wiebe, 2003), and morphological segmentation and analysis (Smit et al., 2014). We present the performance obtained using different confidence parameters b. These plots suggest that our model is robust to different confidence parameters.
source_text: Despite these limitations, we hope that LEAQI provides a useful (and relatively simple) bridge that can enable using rule-based systems, heuristics, and unsupervised models as building blocks for more complex supervised learning systems. This is particularly attractive in settings where we have very strong rule-based systems, ones which often outperform the best statistical systems, like coreference resolution (Lee et al., 2011) , information extraction (Riloff and Wiebe, 2003) , and morphological segmentation and analysis (Smit et al., 2014 . We show the performance of using difference confidence parameters b. These plots indicate that our model is robust to difference confidence parameters.

text: The pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm has achieved substantial success in NLP applications in recent years and has been adopted across a range of NLP tasks (Radford et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2019). Howard and Ruder (2018) proposed pre-training a language model on a large general-domain corpus and then fine-tuning it for the target task with novel techniques such as discriminative fine-tuning, slanted triangular learning rates, and gradual unfreezing. In this way, such pretrained models achieve excellent results even when only limited labeled data are available. Pre-training approaches are often formulated with different objectives, including language modeling (Peters et al., 2018; Howard and Ruder, 2018; Yang et al., 2019b) and masked language modeling (Devlin et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019). Their performance can also be improved by training larger models on more data (Yang et al., 2019b; Liu et al., 2019).
source_text: The pre-training and fine-tuning framework has achieved huge success on NLP applications in recent years, and has been applied to a variety of NLP tasks (Radford et al., 2018; Akbik et al., 2019) . Howard and Ruder (2018) proposed to pre-train a language model on a large general-domain corpus and fine-tune it on the target task using some novel techniques like discriminative fine-tuning, slanted triangular learning rates, and gradual unfreezing. In this manner, such pretrained models show excellent performance even with small amounts of labeled data. Pre-training methods are often designed with different objectives such as language modeling (Peters et al., 2018; Howard and Ruder, 2018; Yang et al., 2019b) and masked language modeling (Devlin et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019) . Their performances are also improved with training larger models on more data (Yang et al., 2019b; Liu et al., 2019) .
